narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and upper-middle family background. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated a 68.9% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first try. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to typical students who average around the middle of their class. Your Year 1 decile, also in the lowest group well below the average, was the next strongest influence pulling the prediction toward failure. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, slightly above the typical applicant's, strongly worked in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting bar exam success. Your undergraduate GPA, though lower than the average of 3.21, still offered a small positive push against failure, as did your family income level. Being a full-time student, like most applicants, had almost no impact either way. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early and later school rankings stand out as notably lower, while your LSAT gives you an edge many don't have. This isn't the end of your path—consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as those proved most critical here, and reapply once you've strengthened those areas. You've already shown resilience; with targeted improvements, you can shift this prediction in your favor. We're rooting for you.
